---
output: github_document
---

<!-- README.md is generated from README.Rmd. Please edit that file -->

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  fig.path = "man/figures/README-",
  out.width = "100%"
)
```

# BOP2basket

<!-- badges: start -->
<!-- badges: end -->

The goal of BOP2basket is to ...

## Installation

You can install the development version of BOP2basket like so:

``` r
# FILL THIS IN! HOW CAN PEOPLE INSTALL YOUR DEV PACKAGE?
```

## Example

This is a basic example which shows you how to solve a common problem:

```{r example}
library(BOP2basket)
## basic example code
```

What is special about using `README.Rmd` instead of just `README.md`? You can include R chunks like so:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

You'll still need to render `README.Rmd` regularly, to keep `README.md` up-to-date. `devtools::build_readme()` is handy for this.

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo = FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

In that case, don't forget to commit and push the resulting figure files, so they display on GitHub and CRAN.

```{r}
library(BOP2basket)

cohortsize <- matrix(rep(5, 3*5), nrow = 3, ncol = 5)

ab <- decidePar(cohortsize, ntype = 5, ntrial = 50,
                p0 = rep(0.2, 5), p1 = rep(0.4, 5),
                var.small = 0.1, var.big = 10)

res <- design(cohortsize, ntype = 5,
              p.true = c(0.2,0.2,0.4,0.2,0.4),
              p.null = rep(0.2, 5),
              ntrial = 100,
              mu.par = 0, v = 0.01,
              a = ab$a, b = ab$b,
              lam = 0.9, gam = 1,
              type1 = 0.1,
              method = "CBHM")
```
